header_summary:
Recent discussions within the Bitcoin development community have centered on enhancing AI supervision and error accountability through blockchain solutions. A significant focus is on the implementation of data-carrying annexes in P2TR transactions, which are proposed to facilitate authenticated data payloads crucial for supervising AI agents within accountable computing contracts. These annexes aim to ensure that AI responses are computationally verifiable, addressing the principal-agent problem by making AI agents bear the cost of errors, thus aligning their incentives with those of human users. The potential applications of these systems, including a hypothetical scenario with Robinson Crusoe and an AI named Alice, underscore the practical challenges and economic implications of AI-agent interactions. This approach is further elaborated through the example of a cryptographers' club seeking to identify an affordable red wine, employing cryptographic tools like zero-knowledge proofs to maintain solution confidentiality and accuracy [source](https://gnusha.org/pi/bitcoindev/CALZpt+GGORG3bgM0C3sQYVNbc1W7aFyb0qP_c2xbZ8f64S_ksQ@mail.gmail.com/T/#u#m49c2ecc7d787093a0fb97de67ccbedde0419eb51).

In parallel, enhancements in wallet software have been discussed, particularly concerning the functionality of transaction replacements through the Replace-By-Fee (RBF) mechanism. This feature, crucial for managing blockchain transactions securely, allows users to replace transactions without a signaling bit, increasing usability while ensuring informed decision-making through clear warnings about potential risks. This adjustment is especially beneficial for transactions from legacy wallets that do not support BIP125 signaling, balancing user-friendliness with the complexities of secure blockchain management [source](https://gnusha.org/pi/bitcoindev/178162039498.7.16213177200751410070.1421232845@slmail.me/T/#mf1437c3fbbe517e3e2976bec1440f07c45312cc7).

Moreover, discussions on data synchronization techniques for Bitcoin have highlighted the preference for Reed-Solomon (RS) codes over fountain codes due to security concerns with potentially malicious peers. RS codes provide a robust solution for error correction in unreliable peer environments, crucial for maintaining network security and anonymity. This choice impacts various protocol decisions, including data shard distribution and node identity management, which are vital for preserving privacy and minimizing traceability within the network [source](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/fountain-codes-a-way-to-reduce-blockchain-storage-costs/2624/3).

recent_posts.title: Accountable Computing On-Chain Contracts for AI Agents Supervision
recent_posts.summary: - Bitcoin development discussions focus on data annexes for P2TR transactions.  - These discussions address AI accuracy and computational verifiability in user interactions.  - Economic and cryptographic challenges in AI contracts remain under exploration.

recent_posts.title: [BIP Proposal] Removal of BIP 125 RBF signalling in wallet transactions
recent_posts.summary:
- The wallet software supports transaction replacements through RBF, with safety warnings included.
- It also aids legacy wallets which may lack BIP125 signaling for safer operations.
- The design balances ease of use with safe, secure transaction management for users.

recent_posts.title: Fountain Codes: a way to reduce blockchain storage costs
recent_posts.summary:
- RS codes are preferred over fountain codes due to security in unreliable peer environments.
- RS codes maintain node anonymity and hinder node distinguishability, enhancing network privacy.
- They facilitate error correction and complexities of node operations, including intellectual property considerations.

recent_posts.title: [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero
recent_posts.summary: - Junhyuk Lee explores Bitcoin security and miner profitability as block rewards decrease.  - He introduces G_t as a threshold for detecting miners' deviations in Bitcoin's network.  - Lee suggests mechanisms like base fees and adaptable block sizes to maintain network stability.

recent_posts.title: Privately running wallet privacy heuristics on your own UTXOs without needing to run your own node
recent_posts.summary:
- Recent tools assess blockchain transaction privacy by analyzing wallet data leakage.
- A new system allows private, node-independent privacy heuristic analysis using encrypted filters.
- This system employs cryptographic techniques and Tor to ensure transaction security and anonymity.

active_posts.title: Prohibit Merkle Internal Node Preimages That Encode Minimal 64-Byte Transactions
active_posts.summary:
- The new Bitcoin rule targets specific 64-byte transaction structures to enhance security.
- Implementation includes a C++ function for checking prohibited Merkle node preimages.
- Miners are guided on preventing rule violations during block construction.

active_posts.title: Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
active_posts.summary: - The proposed BIP360 modification mandates a merkle path in P2MR control blocks.  - This aims to enhance privacy and scripting efficiency by prohibiting depth-zero trees.  - Further consultations will assess the impact on P2MR fungibility and protocol acceptance.

active_posts.title: Proposal discussion: BIP39 native-language display wordlists
active_posts.summary:
- The proposal enhances Bitcoin wallet recovery by adding native language support for BIP39 phrases.
- It aims to keep the original English wordlist, using native languages for better usability.
- Feedback on this multilingual support approach is currently sought via a GitHub repository.

active_posts.title: Public key recovery for EC leaves in P2MR (BIP-360)
active_posts.summary:
- The proposal aims to optimize BIP-360 by introducing a recoverable EC leaf.
- This adjustment reduces witness size by deriving the public key from EC signatures.
- Proposed changes streamline witness validation and enhance security with unique nonce derivation.

active_posts.title: A Bitcoin-native LLM: dataset, architecture and open questions
active_posts.summary:
- A Bitcoin-native LLM is designed to enhance interaction with Bitcoin-specific elements.
- It will use a substantial dataset from diverse Bitcoin-related sources for training.
- Its architecture combines a static model with a live data querying tool layer.

active_posts.title: State of the transaction privacy work in Bitcoin
active_posts.summary:
- Transaction privacy in Bitcoin focuses on sidechains, L2s, and wallets, not Layer 1.
- Cross-Input Signature Aggregation might reduce CoinJoin costs but not enhance privacy.
- Strategic focus shifts towards Bitcoin privacy methods fitting regulatory frameworks and protocol stability.

today_in_history_posts.title: BIP Idea : DDoS resistance via decentrilized proof-of-work
today_in_history_posts.summary: - The author suggests modifying Bitcoin's proof-of-work to secure and decentralize transactions.  - The change aims to make the network resistant to DDoS attacks and enhance its functionality.  - This incentivizes miners to include high-value transactions, promoting a more free-market economy.

today_in_history_posts.title: BOLT 11, real time micro payments, and route redundancy
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- The writer queries the absence of a refund address in BOLT 11 unlike in BIP 70.
- They discuss adapting a fuel pump for Lightning, needing a refund option for pre-payments.
- They express concerns about real-time micropayments' feasibility and network reliability.

today_in_history_posts.title: Deflationary money is a Good Thing
today_in_history_posts.summary: - Passive and active investments differ in management intensity and asset engagement.  - Passive investing issues include asset underutilization and misaligned agent interests.  - Proposing central banks use inflationary currencies backed by deflationary ones like Bitcoin.
